answers.question: List of scenarios
answer: NGFS Hot house world – Current policies
NGFS Hot house world – Nationally determined contributions (NDC)
answers.evidence: The scenarios of the Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) incorporate different degrees of risk along different paths of global warming in selected scenarios: • Orderly (sub-scenarios: Net Zero 2050, Below 2°C)... • Disorderly (sub-scenarios: Delayed transition, Divergent net zero)... • Hot house world (sub-scenarios: Current policies, Nationally determined contributions)... The NGFS scenarios are linked to Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) scenarios, which Swiss Re uses for natural catastrophe re/insurance modelling... eg 8.5W/m2 for RCP8.5, the worst-case scenario... RCP4.5 is an intermediate scenario reflecting current policy actions.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63–64; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.6–7
answers.notes: NGFS scenarios used for investments; RCP scenarios used for underwriting/natural catastrophe modelling. Both documents list the same scenarios.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Multiple temperature outcomes are described: (1) Below 2°C / Net Zero 2050 (Orderly NGFS scenarios); (2) 1.5°C pathway (referenced for investment decarbonisation targets); (3) Unmitigated warming under Hot house world / RCP8.5 (severe physical risks, irreversible impacts). The long-term horizon for life & health analysis is explicitly stated as 2100. No specific numerical temperature overshoot figure is stated for the Hot house world scenario.
answers.evidence: Orderly (sub-scenarios: Net Zero 2050, Below 2°C)... Hot house world (sub-scenarios: Current policies, Nationally determined contributions): Some climate policies are implemented in some jurisdictions, but global efforts to limit global warming are insufficient. This leads to severe physical risks including irreversible impacts... Swiss Re does not expect any of the RCP climate scenarios it has analysed to have a financially material net adverse impact on its life and health portfolio in the long-term, ie 2100... Swiss Re's emission reduction target for the corporate bond and listed equity portfolio is based on the IPCC 1.5°C pathway scenarios.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63–64, 66; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.7
answers.notes: The documents reference 1.5°C and below 2°C targets explicitly. RCP8.5 is described as the worst-case scenario with no mitigation but no specific end-of-century temperature number is given for it.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Two scenario providers are explicitly named: (1) NGFS (Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the Financial System) — for the NGFS scenarios used in investments and as the overall narrative; (2) IPCC (Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change) — referenced for the RCP scenarios and 1.5°C pathway. The RCP scenarios are used off-the-shelf from the scientific literature.
answers.evidence: The scenarios of the Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) serve as the common narrative for Swiss Re's overall scenario analysis and are used for investments. For underwriting portfolios, scenario analyses are based on the relevant scientific literature and Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) scenarios... Swiss Re's emission reduction target for the corporate bond and listed equity portfolio is based on the IPCC 1.5°C pathway scenarios.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63, 66; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.6
answers.notes: NGFS is the primary named scenario provider. IPCC is referenced for the 1.5°C pathway and as the scientific basis for RCP scenarios. An unnamed external data provider is also mentioned for investment scenario analysis (footnote 39/5).

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: The NGFS scenarios are linked to Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) scenarios, which Swiss Re uses for natural catastrophe re/insurance modelling. RCP scenarios represent possible future concentration trajectories of greenhouse gases. The scenarios are named after the resulting radiative forcing at the end of the 21st century, eg 8.5W/m2 for RCP8.5...
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.7
answers.notes: The documents use NGFS scenarios (which are linked to RCPs) and standalone RCP scenarios. No SSP identifiers (e.g. SSP1-2.6, SSP5-8.5) are mentioned anywhere in either document. There is no mixing of SSP and RCP families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — partially. The NGFS and RCP scenarios are used as standard frameworks, but Swiss Re adapts the hazard module of its proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework to the projected climate under different RCP scenarios. The analysis is applied to specific perils (US tropical cyclones, UK floods) using static portfolios reflecting the overall market.
answers.evidence: The most recent analysis focused on tropical cyclones (TC) in the US, as well as floods in the UK. This selection is based on the economic relevance for the Swiss Re Group (based on AEL) and the expected impact of climate change on these specific perils. The analysis was conducted by adjusting the hazard module in Swiss Re's proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework to the projected climate in the long term, ie 2050, while keeping all other modules and assumptions constant (ie vulnerability, exposure and insurance conditions).
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63
answers.notes: The document does not use the words "customised", "downscaled", "localised" or "tailored" explicitly. However, the methodology describes adjusting a proprietary model's hazard module to RCP-projected climate conditions for specific perils and geographies, which constitutes adaptation beyond off-the-shelf use.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: Swiss Re uses qualitative and quantitative scenario analyses to assess the resilience of its strategy under different climate scenarios. The scenarios of the Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) serve as the common narrative for Swiss Re's overall scenario analysis and are used for investments. For underwriting portfolios, scenario analyses are based on the relevant scientific literature and Representative Concentration Pathway (RCP) scenarios.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.6
answers.notes: No language such as "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", or "collaborative development" of scenarios is found in either document. Swiss Re adopts externally developed NGFS and RCP scenarios.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — partially. A 2023 baseline period is used for the natural catastrophe scenario analysis (projecting to 2050). For the investment portfolio, a 2018 base year is used for GHG intensity targets. The NGFS "Current policies" sub-scenario within Hot house world functions implicitly as a reference/baseline scenario. No single explicitly labelled "baseline scenario" is defined for comparison across all analyses.
answers.evidence: This pilot scenario study was based on a different baseline period (2005) and projection horizon (2055) than the new assessment (2023 baseline and 2050 projection)... Hot house world (sub-scenarios: Current policies, Nationally determined contributions): Some climate policies are implemented in some jurisdictions, but global efforts to limit global warming are insufficient.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63 (footnote 40); Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.7
answers.notes: The document uses a 2023 baseline period for the physical risk scenario analysis. The "Current policies" NGFS sub-scenario serves as a de facto reference scenario. No single document section explicitly labels a "baseline scenario" for comparison purposes.

answers.question: Model used
answer: Swiss Re proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework (four modules: hazard, vulnerability, exposure, insurance conditions)
answers.evidence: Swiss Re's proprietary natural catastrophe loss modelling framework consists of four modules: hazard (location, frequency and intensity of events), vulnerability (extent of damage at a given event intensity), exposure (location and value of the insured objects) and insurance conditions (proportion of loss insured). All relevant loss drivers are directly incorporated into the respective module... The analysis was conducted by adjusting the hazard module in Swiss Re's proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework to the projected climate in the long term, ie 2050.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.59, 63
answers.notes: No named third-party IAMs (e.g. REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM) or macroeconomic models (e.g. NiGEM) are identified. An unnamed "external data provider" is referenced for investment scenario analysis using NGFS Phase II scenarios. The only explicitly named model is Swiss Re's own proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: Projected annual changes in AEL for US tropical cyclones are in the range of –0.5% to 1.1%; and 0.2% to 1.4% for UK floods under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5 scenarios... Physical risks are relevant where assets are exposed to weather-related perils, in particular acute, event-driven impacts such as tropical cyclones and floods. These can result in direct damage to assets or loss of revenue due to business interruption unless insurance protection is in place.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63, 65
answers.notes: The scenario analysis explicitly quantifies projected changes in annual expected losses (AEL) from acute physical risks (tropical cyclones, floods) under RCP4.5 and RCP8.5. Gross AEL data by peril and region is also provided in the sustainability data appendix.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: Rising sea levels... More frequent and severe rainfall: Rising temperatures enable the atmosphere to hold more moisture, thus increasing the severity and frequency of rainfall events... Increased temperature extremes... Hot and dry conditions exacerbate drought and wildfire risk... Transition risk analysis considers projections for investee companies' GHG emissions and carbon pricing as key components. Physical risk analysis focuses on the evolution of climate-related perils by location for the remainder of the century... The scenarios are named after the resulting radiative forcing at the end of the 21st century, eg 8.5W/m2 for RCP8.5.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.59, 65; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.7
answers.notes: Variables are drawn from multiple sections of both documents covering physical risk drivers for underwriting and investment portfolios.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: The most recent analysis focused on tropical cyclones (TC) in the US, as well as floods in the UK. This selection is based on the economic relevance for the Swiss Re Group (based on AEL) and the expected impact of climate change on these specific perils. The analysis was conducted by adjusting the hazard module in Swiss Re's proprietary natural catastrophe loss-modelling framework to the projected climate in the long term, ie 2050... Physical risk analysis focuses on the evolution of climate-related perils by location for the remainder of the century... Swiss Re identifies, monitors, analyses and prioritises short-term threats from relevant man-made risks and natural hazards, including climate-related risks, across its operations at the global, regional and local levels.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63, 65, 69
answers.notes: Global NGFS scenarios are used for investments. For underwriting, the analysis is applied at a regional/local level (US tropical cyclones, UK floods). Physical risk analysis for investments also considers perils by location.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Three time horizons are defined: Short term = next five years; Medium term = up to ten years; Long term = over ten years (with further specification by line of business for underwriting; for life & health, long term is defined as up to 2100; for property re/insurance, long term is defined as 2050). The rationale for these timeframes is partially explained: the short-term horizon aligns with re/insurance contract duration; the long-term horizon of 2050 is used for physical risk projections; 2100 is used for life & health because premium rates are not reviewed annually in those contracts.
answers.evidence: This section outlines the actual and potential physical and transition risks identified by Swiss Re in the short term (next five years), medium term (up to ten years) and long term (over ten years). For underwriting, long term is further defined based on line-of-business-specific requirements... The time horizons considered are short term (over the next five years), medium term (up to ten years) as well as the long term (2050)... Swiss Re does not expect any of the RCP climate scenarios it has analysed to have a financially material net adverse impact on its life and health portfolio in the long-term, ie 2100.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.58; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.7, 10
answers.notes: The NZ CRD report specifies long term as 2050 for the risk management section, while the Group report and NZ report both use 2100 for life & health. Rationale is partially provided (contract duration, line-of-business requirements) but not fully elaborated for each horizon.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.answer: Yes — partial justification is provided. The NGFS scenarios are described as serving as the "common narrative" for overall scenario analysis. The selection of specific perils (US tropical cyclones, UK floods) for the natural catastrophe analysis is explicitly justified by economic relevance (based on AEL) and expected climate change impact. No broader justification is given for why NGFS was chosen over other scenario frameworks.
answers.evidence: The scenarios of the Network of Central Banks and Supervisors for Greening the Financial System (NGFS) serve as the common narrative for Swiss Re's overall scenario analysis and are used for investments... The most recent analysis focused on tropical cyclones (TC) in the US, as well as floods in the UK. This selection is based on the economic relevance for the Swiss Re Group (based on AEL) and the expected impact of climate change on these specific perils.
answers.location_hint: Swiss Re Group Sustainability Report 2024, p.63; Swiss Re NZ CRD Report 2024, p.6
answers.notes: The justification for peril/geography selection is explicit. The justification for choosing NGFS as the overarching framework is limited to describing it as the "common narrative" — no deeper rationale (e.g. regulatory alignment, comparability) is stated in the documents.
